summary: This week’s agentic AI signal is unusually concrete: coding agents are escaping the demo phase and forcing decisions about sandbox boundaries, review surfaces, and cost discipline. The fun stuff is still there — Ableton MCP, Git for AI agents, desktop CLIs, HF agent spaces — but the strongest through-line is operational: teams want agents that can act, remember, and recover without quietly wrecking a repo or burning money through fragile browser loops.

highlights.title: DeepClaude – Claude Code agent loop with DeepSeek V4 Pro
highlights.why: The biggest builder signal of the week: people want Claude Code-style autonomous coding loops, but they also want them to be model-agnostic, cheaper, and hackable.

highlights.title: Computer Use is 45x more expensive than structured APIs
highlights.why: A sharp reality check for browser agents: GUI control is compelling, but teams are increasingly realizing the production win often comes from structured tools, not pixel-driving demos.

highlights.title: Running Codex safely at OpenAI
highlights.meta: OpenAI RSS · sandboxing, approvals, network policies
highlights.why: Exactly the right kind of boring-important agent infrastructure: if coding agents are going to touch real repos and networks, safety policy becomes a product surface, not an internal footnote.

highlights.title: Advancing voice intelligence with new models in the API
highlights.why: Voice agents are getting more practical: lower-latency reasoning, translation, and transcription makes phone, assistant, and multimodal agent loops much easier to ship.

highlights.title: AlphaEvolve: Gemini-powered coding agent scaling impact across fields
highlights.why: AlphaEvolve pushes the coding-agent pattern beyond software: search, code generation, and feedback loops are becoming a general recipe for optimization and scientific work.

highlights.title: Anthropic: Agents for financial services
highlights.why: Good evidence that enterprise agents are moving from generic demos to verticalized, connector-heavy workflows where approvals and compliance matter as much as model quality.

highlights.title: Show HN: Ableton Live MCP
highlights.why: One of the most delightful MCP stories in a while: it shows the protocol spreading from dev tooling into creative software, where agent control actually feels magical instead of bureaucratic.

highlights.title: Show HN: Git for AI Agents
highlights.why: A surprisingly strong practitioner signal: developers increasingly feel normal VCS is missing agent-native primitives like reasoning history, action lineage, and easier recovery from bad autonomous edits.

highlights.title: Agent-harness-kit scaffolding for multi-agent workflows (MCP, provider-agnostic)
highlights.why: The ecosystem is clearly asking for harnesses, not just agents: reusable scaffolding for tools, approvals, and orchestration is becoming part of the real stack.

highlights.title: smolagents/ml-intern
highlights.why: Useful Hugging Face signal because it reflects what people actually want to try right now: interactive agent workflows that feel like a tiny teammate, not just another static demo.

highlights.title: Claude Code CVE-2026-39861: sandbox escape via symlink
highlights.why: A reminder that coding-agent security is now real security: once the agent can act on your machine, sandbox design and file-boundary bugs become headline-worthy.

highlights.title: Gemini API File Search is now multimodal
highlights.meta: Google Keyword · multimodal RAG with citations
highlights.why: Grounding is still half the battle. Better file search, filters, and page-level citations improve the boring-but-essential substrate for agents that need to retrieve and then act.

items.title: DeepClaude – Claude Code agent loop with DeepSeek V4 Pro
items.why: Massive interest in a cheaper, open-ish take on the coding-agent loop people now expect from Claude Code.

items.title: Computer Use is 45x more expensive than structured APIs
items.why: A high-signal cost argument against naive browser automation as the default agent architecture.

items.title: AlphaEvolve: Gemini-powered coding agent scaling impact across fields
items.why: A strong example of the coding-agent pattern expanding into broader scientific and optimization workflows.

items.title: The agent harness belongs outside the sandbox
items.why: One of the week’s best architectural debates about where agent control, credentials, and side effects should actually live.

items.title: Show HN: Ableton Live MCP
items.why: Shows MCP escaping pure devtools and becoming a way to make creative software agent-addressable.

items.title: Show HN: Agent-desktop – Native desktop automation CLI for AI agents
items.why: Desktop control keeps broadening the meaning of computer-use agents beyond browser tabs alone.

items.title: Show HN: Git for AI Agents
items.why: People want agent-native workflow history and easier rollback, not just more autonomous code generation.

items.title: GETadb.com – every GET request creates a DB
items.why: A fun vibe-coding primitive: make backend creation trivial enough that an agent can wire something up fast, then argue about security immediately after.

items.title: Show HN: Runs AI coding agents inside isolated Docker containers
items.why: Even low-traffic launches are clustering around the same practical concern: coding agents need isolation by default.

items.title: Show HN: Agentctl, a local control plane for coding agents
items.why: Tiny thread, strong signal: permissions, secrets, and outbound-call policy are rapidly becoming a real product layer around agents.

items.title: Selvedge – an MCP server that captures why AI agents change code
items.why: Audit trails for agent edits are becoming a category of their own — people want to preserve rationale, not just diffs.

items.title: Anyone else losing tokens to hallucinated MCP tool calls in production?
items.why: Small thread, very real problem: tool schemas and namespace discipline are now an operational concern because wasted calls directly translate into spend and brittleness.

items.title: Running Codex safely at OpenAI
items.why: One of the clearest public writeups this week on agent sandboxing, approvals, and network policy.

items.title: Advancing voice intelligence with new models in the API
items.why: Voice IO keeps becoming more agent-shaped: reasoning plus realtime speech is a better foundation for assistants that actually do things.

items.title: Anthropic: Higher usage limits for Claude and a compute deal with SpaceX
items.why: Higher ceilings matter because long-running coding and research agents fail just as often on quota and context economics as on raw model quality.

items.title: Anthropic: Agents for financial services
items.why: Concrete, regulated vertical-agent packaging is a stronger market signal than another generic benchmark win.

items.title: Gemini API File Search is now multimodal
items.why: Grounded retrieval is still the substrate for serious agents, and multimodal search with citations is a practical upgrade.

items.title: Mistral: Remote agents in Vibe. Powered by Mistral Medium 3.5.
items.why: A useful product signal even slightly outside the week: async cloud-run coding agents and CLI-to-cloud handoff are clearly where UX is converging.

items.title: google/gemma-4-31B-it-assistant
items.why: Strong open-model demand for assistant-style, function-calling-capable systems that can slot into agent loops.

items.title: smolagents/ml-intern
items.why: Good proxy for what excites builders right now: lightweight, interactive agent experiences with obvious next-step affordances.

tangentRadar.whyItMatters: The category is rapidly maturing from write code for me into a full stack question about review, rollback, quotas, and execution boundaries.
signals: DeepClaude — huge HN demand for model-agnostic coding loops

tangentRadar.whyItMatters: The most useful agent improvements this week were not pure model jumps; they were better tool surfaces, policies, and grounded retrieval layers.
signals: Computer Use is 45x more expensive than structured APIs
Gemini API File Search is now multimodal
Hallucinated MCP tool calls in production

tangentRadar.whyItMatters: MCP still has momentum, but the community conversation is shifting from hype to specific questions about explainability, overhead, and when simpler wrappers are enough.
Voice agents are becoming less toy-like because speech, translation, and retrieval are getting folded into the same action loop instead of sitting beside it.
A recurring subtext this week: people do not just want autonomous agents, they want ones that can preserve rationale, recover gracefully, and avoid repeating mistakes.
signals: Sandbox and rollback tooling around coding agents

tangentRadar.whyItMatters: Cost, sandbox safety, and tool-call correctness are turning into de facto eval dimensions, which is healthier than judging agents on demo fluency alone.
signals: Production complaints about hallucinated MCP calls

experimentQueue.title: Price the browser-agent tax
experimentQueue.why: The strongest practical critique this week was about computer-use cost and fragility versus structured APIs.
experimentQueue.prompt: Take one task you currently run through browser automation and benchmark it against a structured-tool version on cost, latency, and failure rate.

experimentQueue.title: Add an approval boundary, not just a better model
experimentQueue.why: OpenAI, Anthropic, and the community all pointed at the same thing: the harness matters.
experimentQueue.prompt: Insert a single human approval gate before shell, network, or destructive file actions and measure how much quality you lose versus how much trust you gain.

experimentQueue.title: Try an agent-native review surface
experimentQueue.why: Git for AI Agents, Stage-style tooling, and rationale capture all suggest that review UX is now part of the agent stack.
experimentQueue.prompt: Run one coding task with normal git diff review and one with explicit rationale / action-history capture, then compare how quickly you can audit mistakes.

experimentQueue.title: Prototype a multimodal voice workflow
experimentQueue.why: Voice and multimodal retrieval are getting just good enough to turn into useful operating surfaces for agents.
experimentQueue.prompt: Build a thin loop that listens, retrieves a cited artifact, and proposes an action — then test whether it feels faster than keyboard-first orchestration.

method: Manual curation from verified Hacker News Algolia story metadata for high-signal agent posts in the last 7 days.
Official vendor verification via accessible feeds/pages, including OpenAI News RSS and public lab announcement pages from Anthropic, Google, and Mistral.
Hugging Face trending models/spaces used as lightweight builder-interest signals.
Reddit was blocked by CAPTCHA in this run, so the community section intentionally falls back to accessible HN and GitHub-linked discussion rather than fabricating Reddit items.
